title: Honest scorecard from running 6 AI-driven strategies in public. Five are up. One is down 23%.
author: altindexAI
selftext:
I'm the founder of AltIndex. Before I get to the scorecard, a quick explanation of what I'm running and why I'm running it publicly.

AltIndex is an alternative-data stock screener. The core of it is an AI score, zero to one hundred, that combines three sub-scores for every stock we track. Employment pulls from job-posting volume, hiring velocity, and employee sentiment. Audience pulls from Reddit mentions, social sentiment, web traffic, and app downloads. Fundamental pulls from earnings, margins, and the usual balance-sheet and valuation inputs. Those three sub-scores roll up into a single top-line AI score per stock. The picks and the strategies below are all built on that score or variations of it. I won't post the exact weighting because some of it is proprietary and some of it gets tuned, but the sub-scores are all visible in the product.

The public marketing claim on the homepage is a roughly 75% win rate across picks. That's the kind of claim every screener makes, which is exactly why I wanted to put it on the line in public. Backtests are cheap. If the algorithm is real, it should work forward in time with timestamped picks that anyone can audit months later. That's the only test I trust, and it's the whole reason these six paper portfolios exist.

Six strategies, live since early 2026, $10K starting capital each.

Top 5, which tracks the five highest-AI-scored stocks, is up 23.55%. Congress Copy, which mirrors politician buys, is up 20.63%. WW3 ETF, a defense and oil macro hedge, is up 6.95%. AltIndex 50, a broader AI-scored basket, is up 5.92%. Reddit Top 10, built on social-signal buzz, is up 1.85%. Top 5 Robotics is down 23.17%.

Aggregate comes out around +6%, roughly S&P-parity over the same window. That looks fine on a summary line until you see the number covers a bimodal distribution. Two strategies are generating all the alpha. One strategy is eating a lot of it.

The robotics drawdown deserves honesty. The thesis was always long-duration, three to five years before the hardware buildout plays out in revenue. A 23% drawdown over a few months on a thesis like that is noise if the sizing is right. Mine wasn't. The lesson here is about position sizing. The underlying thesis is fine, the weighting was wrong. Rebalancing that one down is the next thing I'm doing.

Structurally the portfolio is working. Two alpha engines, two support legs, one neutral sleeve, one high-variance bet. The logic is sound. The weighting needs rework.

How are you sizing long-duration thematic bets when the short-term drawdown is ugly? Fixed-fractional, conviction-weighted, volatility-targeted? I'd rather hear what's worked for people than keep guessing.
